title: 2025/12/01 - SSC (Part 2)
displayTitle: 2025-12-01 - Social Services and Community Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Disabled People [Part 2]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Disabled People
description: Monday 1 December 2025 | Social Services and Community Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Disabled People This video includes live interpreting. The goal is to provide clear and accurate information, but some details may be missed or contain errors. It is shared to give you access to the information, but please double-check any details before relying on them. Hearing of evidence: Ministry for Disabled People – Whaikaha Paula Tesoriero, Chief Executive Ginny Baddeley, Deputy Chief Executive Strategy and Enablement Ben O’Meara, Deputy Chief Executive Policy and Insights Rebecca Elvy, Deputy Chief Executive Outreach and Innovation Ronelle Baker, Kaihautū – Chief Advisor Māori

Alright. Well, uh, good morning and welcome to the Social Services and Community Select Committee. Uh, so this morning we have, uh, ministry for Disabled People, um, 60 minutes. And what I might do is ask you to first introduce yourselves and then make some introductory comments. Uh, I will also make the observation that we have interpreters assisting us who are online, and so they'll ask everyone to just remember that and, uh, try to, you know, not talk too fast and allow time, uh, for the interchange of answers and questions and answers as we go with that.
Um, thank you. Thank You, Mr. Chair. I was going to begin by making that same point about our NZSL interpreters,

the Ministry of Disabled People. My name's Paula. I'm the Secretary for Disabled People and Chief Executive of the Ministry. My signed name is Two Fists, moving in a forward direction like bicycle, reflecting my past as a Paralympic cyclist. Uh, and I'm sitting with colleagues wearing a, uh, light blue jacket and I have dark blonde short hair swep to one side.

I'm joined today by my colleagues Ben O Mirror, deputy Chief Executive Policy and Insights. Jenny Badley, deputy CE Strategy and Enablement. Rebecca Alvy, deputy C Outreach and Innovation, and Elle Baker Katu, chief Advisor Maori. So today is our birthday.

It's been one year since the ministry became a standalone public service department, as you've indicated, Mr. Chair, I'll just spend a few minutes, uh, outlining some key things that we have been doing over the past year before handing over to the committee for questions. We've been really focused on building a strong foundation since the machinery of government changes and shifting to our new structure and operating model has allowed us to redefine how we can drive real and meaningful change for disabled people.

Our new role means looking across sectors and thinking both short and long term about the outcomes that matter. To do this, we are focused on six strategic outcome areas across accessibility, education, employment, health, housing, and justice.

These shape our priorities and work program, which include firstly the refresh of the New Zealand disability strategy. We launched tomorrow morning and sets the direction or disability across government for the next five years. Last month, we launched the New Zealand Sign Language Strategy to ensure that NZSL thrives in.

We have also made progress with other government agencies on implementing the recommendations that were made to the government by the United Nations Convention on Rights of Persons with Disabilities Committee, we've continued to provide advice on the Royal Commission into abuse and care response.

To shape the government's response, we have firmed up and started delivering on our accessibility work program, including strengthening the alternate format service across government. We have invested in initiatives to create employment opportunities in the public service, including our recently launched cross agency summer internship program for disabled students, which saw 13 disabled students placed across 13 government agencies.

And we've continued to build our ministry to achieve our vision. We also support cross government priorities by providing second opinion policy advice, offering disability expertise, and brokering opportunities for disabled people to influence policy. As part of our stewardship role, we keep an overview of and contribute to major issues such as transport and technology developments.

Essential to all of this is the ministry's work alongside disabled people, t Maori and their whanau to drive meaningful change their perspectives, experience and voice are essential. Everybody in ROA has a role in improving the lives of disabled people.

And so we will continue to build relationships across central and local government, business and industry and the community to make change happen. Our ability to deliver our work rests on a strong and well performing ministry. While disabled people still face many barriers, we are now in an excellent, strong position to accelerate progress.

Our vision remains clear, disabled people thriving in New Zealand. I'm happy to take your questions. Thank you. Um, first question. Thank you. Um, thank you for that overview and, um, the work that you're doing. Um, I've got a question around the employment side of things.

You've mentioned the initiatives within the public service seen that in the annual report as well. Um, you will also know that the unemployment figure, the June quarter shows the unemployment figure for disabled people is 14.1% the highest it's ever been since it was recorded in 2017.

Um, you would've also heard from disabled people that many are struggling to put food on the table and to, um, access the basic necessities of life. What employment initiatives are underway that are new that, uh, f Kaha is supporting or investing in, um, to shift those figures?

Your performance measures are just around the employment rate going up under utilization going down and the pay gap going down. Um, but what are the initiatives that will get us there? Thank you for that question. So, um, there are a number of things that we are doing. Um, so you are right.

The employment gap between disabled people and non-disabled people in New Zealand is too wide, and that's due to a range of things. It's around lack of reasonable accommodation and supports for disabled people. It can be related to transport and wider infrastructure.

Um, we know that disabled people are less likely to have a post post school qualification, and we know that there continue to be attitude. So those are some of the barriers to employment. The things that we are doing are as follows. Firstly, uh, members will see tomorrow when the New Zealand disability Strategy is released, that there are a number of actions that ministers have agreed to around employment, uh, which we are confident will help with narrowing that gap.

Um, members may have seen some of those draft actions in the strategy. Uh, the final strategy comes out tomorrow. I touched briefly on the internship program across the public service. That is really important because the public service has an opportunity to be a real role model in this space. This is the first time that we have gone across government with such an internship program with 10 agencies, um, taking disabled young people and giving them a foot in the door.

Some of those people had not worked for years and were just looking for that opportunity. The next thing that we will be launching in the first part of next calendar year is our employment website. So what we know from our engagement, both in the public sector and private sector, is that actually most employers want to do the right thing.

Where some of the difficulties arise is in having access to good information and good advice in a good suite of policies. So we are responding to that by launching an employment website in the first part of next year, and we'll be doing a lot of, um, promotional activity around that. And that's intended to be a, a, um, support base for employers.

We are also, um, working much more closely with the private sector than we've had the opportunity to do in the past. And so working with organizations, um, to really lift that number. We've also started working with the Institute of Directors and shaping up a work program around governance and other opportunities.

So there is a lot to be done in the, uh, New Zealand disability strategy. You will see that there are a range of, um, indicators to measure success over the next five years. So those are the things that, uh, we are doing in leading most of that work, uh, as well as contributing to other government initiatives.

Um, are you aware that organizations that have a government contract to place disabled people into work have had a change in their contract obligations such that they can only have 10% of their clientele who are non beneficiaries, thus impacting a number of people who may not receive a government benefit and want work who cannot be placed into work?

I'm not aware of that specific change, but we are really focused on closing that employment gap between disabled people and non-disabled people. The trend has been, uh, that gap has trended the same for a very long time now, and it is really important.

Our minister holds the employment portfolio as well as disability, and it's an area of, um, great interest to her. It's great to see you all this morning. Thank you for being here. So, um, wanting to know what assurances you can provide that f Kaha is genuinely making progress on its core mandate to drive meaningful change for disabled people when the ministry's current performance measures focused largely on internal policy advice quality and do not yet include clear measurable indicators, especially for outcomes

that rely on coordinated actions across other government agencies, for example, housing, education and, um, and jobs. Thank you for that question. Um, you are right. There are only two performance measures, uh, when a new agency is a is established members maybe where you have a relatively short period of time to develop your statement of strategic contentions.

And we didn't have sufficient time to develop longer term, uh, indicators, cases. The, um, so the two that are, that we've reported on in our annual report, um, next year we will be reporting on more performance measures. Some of the things to bear in mind, um, around demonstrating that outcomes for disabled people, uh, hopefully improve, uh, work that, um, supports the outcomes framework for the New Zealand disability strategy.

So what you'll see tomorrow is a very clear outcomes framework that shows what the current data indicates and then where we expect that to go over the next five years. So that's really important and it will be very clear when the implementation plan comes out next calendar year, which agencies are responsible for that.

And VA Kaha has a key role in leading or contributing to that. The second, um, set of measures that I think are important to be aware of is I talked briefly about the recommendations that the government is implementing that the UN recommended to New Zealand in 2022.

Those actions all sit on the human rights monitor, that the Ministry of Justice, um, administers, and people can go into that and will be updated again early next year with where those different actions are at. And that's an important contribution in terms of measuring better outcomes. Just a couple of other very specific things that we, we are doing.

Um, we have worked really hard to improve the quality of data and evidence around disabled people in New Zealand. And one of the things we've done is join the Social Investment agency mahi tahi data and analytics platform so that we can start to have interactive dashboards and share the progress of disabled people in New Zealand.

And we also fund research, um, that is, uh, undertaken by the Donald Beasley, um, institute who look at particular areas of outcomes for disabled people. Mm-hmm. So the long and short of that, um, is this is a moment in time where we only have those two performance measures.

They will increase in our annual reporting next year, as well as all of the other more external reporting things that I touched on. Oh, thank you so much. It's great to hear that that's coming and we look forward to seeing it. So with those, um, the strategy coming out tomorrow and those expected, um, things to be published next year, we can expect to see that whole of government performance framework that clearly links fa kaha work and the work of partner agencies to measurable improvements in the lives of disabled people in their whanau.

Fantastic. Um, I've just got a couple more subs. So what steps are being taken to encourage agencies to work in accordance with enabling good lives principles and that approach for across government? So in our strategic documents, we talk about being guided by, uh, t and also the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with disabilities and the principles of enabling good lives.

And last year the government, um, uh, expressed its continued support for the principles of enabling good lives. We, um, meet with the National Enabling Good Lives group. We are just right now in the process of agreeing, um, a training program for our staff so that, um, we can make sure that when we are working across government, we can talk about enabling good lives principles to the same extent as we can the UN CRPD in our policy work across government.

So, um, in the period under review, we've provided advice on a significant number of, um, policies that agencies, um, have consulted with us on. That's, you know, part of our key role and we often talk about the principles of enabling good lives, um, the eight principles and the importance of those principles and the lives of disabled people.

Thank you. I've Just got, I'm gonna stop on that. Sure. Um, will, you mentioned meeting with the NGL group. Um, is it true that they're going to be downgraded and will no longer be a ministerial advisory group? I, We are working through with the National Enabling Good Lives Group at the moment, uh, what our relationship agreement looks like with them.

What's the timeframe on that? Um, we are working through, in the short term, getting the training program established and then looking longer term what other key pieces of work we can do together. But one of the things that I'm really conscious of, um, is the number of disabled peoples organizations and representatives groups, um, family groups who want to engage with vaha and we work really hard to make sure that we have good relationships and agreements in place.

So you can't rule out that they won't be downgraded and will no longer be a ministerial advisory group. That's a decision for the minister. Thank You. I a supplementary just on the, um, what you said before about the, uh, IDI and the, uh, social Investment Agency agency, we, we, we heard from them this morning, um, and just really interested in, in the, uh, relationship into play as much as it goes.

And, but between your, um, department and the Social investment agency and the IDI, you know, the collection of data and, and the expression of that back to sort of dashboarding to communities and also to decision makers, how, how is that relationship, um, shaping up? Yeah, thanks Mr. Chair. Early work. Um, but we have a really strong relationship with the Social investment agency.

I touched before that we are part of that mahi um, platform. So we are able to share our data and create those interactive, um, dashboards. And one of the things that we know in New Zealand is that we need to significantly improve the quality and availability of data and respect of disabled people.

And so we'll be drawing on that information as well as survey information, um, to report against the New Zealand disability strategy. We also work with the social investment agency generally around, um, what kinds of initiatives and longer term sort of planning can we think about so that we are supporting disabled people early for better longer term outcomes.

Thank you. You a supplementary. Thank you. Um, Yeah, So with, um, the reporting, I was quite just surprised to see that, um, it was simply recording the directional trends of like up and down. Can we, um, expect to have more fulsome, um, or different, um, ways of reporting that data in next year's annual re report?

Like, like you're saying, it was set up, really the change happened really quickly, this was part of that change. So, um, yeah, having up and down doesn't quite provide the transparency around the government agencies collectively improving the outcomes for the, um, 850,000 New Zealanders, um, living with a disability.

Disability. Yeah. Yeah. So, um, the short answer is yes, we'll be working to refine and provide more granular information. The extent to which we can do that critically depends on the quality of administrative data that is available across governments. And so that's why we are also putting quite a lot of effort into, um, across government community of practice and disability and data evidence and working group to lift the quality of data, um, available across government to, to better understand outcomes for disabled people.

And we also rely on general social, um, or general survey, sorry, information. So what we are really pleased to see now, and this has been increasing in, in the last sort of five or so years, is the number of general surveys, which include questions related to disability. So we are getting a much richer picture from survey data and we need to lift the quality of administrative data so that we can give a more desegregated, granular set of measures.

So that's our intention rather than a up or down. So our work within with, um, the social investment agency has a particular focus on identifying, uh, markers for disability within administrative data so that we can do more granular, granular and detailed analysis, um, based on particular groups.

So it might be learning disabled, it might be people, uh, who have sensory disabilities, um, those sorts of pieces of work. And we need to develop, uh, what are described as code modules to enable that more detailed work to happen. Thank you. Um, another supplemental on that point actually, because just, uh, visiting a lot of my schools, you know, personally across my, um, across my region there, there's obviously a very significant cohort of students who have a variety of, uh, learning disabilities.

Mm-hmm. And one of the challenges is certainly identification. The other one is the appropriate resources, um, to assist with that. Uh, our is is some of your policy works, obviously there's crossover, probably social investment to some degree, um, ministry of Education. How much, how much of that work is sort of sits within your bucket of sort of responsibility, so to speak, and how much is that a relationship with the Ministry of Education, for example?

Yeah, we've, we've got a really, um, strong relationship with the Ministry of Education. Um, the Ministry of Education, we're involved in the New Zealand disability Strategy working group, um, as were officials from Fa Kaha and the community and, and other experts, um, who looked at a series of actions, uh, which will be revealed tomorrow, um, that talk about how to lift, um, uh, opportunities for disabled young people in education.

Key to that as well is lifting performance and quality of data. And so we have been working really hard with the Ministry of Education on that. Thank you. And your question, Paula, Um, uh, thank you firstly for all the work that you do and, and I'm Marie, um, I'm eager to see, uh, progress going forward, but just going back to, uh, the restructure and the focus on, on change processes and, um, could I just ask quickly how that would've impacted on your 20 24, 25 work, uh, program?

Um, and then following that, um, would there have been, would that have been a reason for underspend, uh, on some of your work programs and how did that, um, all work out? Yeah, thank you for that question. So the short answer is yes, we did have an underspend, um, of 4.38 million.

Um, the significant portion of that funding was funding given to us to establish the ministry. Um, so around accessible properties technology, for example, um, we do have approval to transfer that into the current financial year. And so we have a, a particular project aimed at, um, spending that money.

So the underspend is largely, uh, it is due to just not having the runway, um, to spend that money. Uh, we have a very comprehensive work program across the six areas that I touched on, um, to make the greatest use of, of those public funds this year.

Yeah. Um, um, just to reflect back, it's reflected in the report, but I just wanted to come back to, uh, your, um, some of your early discussion around, uh, the underpinned by tgi. Um, so can you, can the ministry, can you provide some concrete examples of actions taken to uphold to Titi, and then just a sub on that and what measurable indicators, uh, exist to track the equity improvements of that based on the six that you referred To Gil?

Thank you for that question. So I think first and foremost, the evidence continues to show the disparity between, um, disabled people and tongue kaha Maori. So there are a range of things that, um, we are doing, uh, to, um, honor our obligations under which, uh, as an agency, you know, like we, I take my responsibilities really seriously.

Key to that has been building a broader set of relationships. So we have, um, involved t Maori in all of our major work streams, so on advisory groups and in our key projects like the New Zealand disability, uh, strategy, like our accessibility advisory group and others.

We have also, um, broadened our relationship to this year establishing a relationship with the Iwi Leaders Forum, um, and working to strengthen our relationships with t Kaha Maori groups. We support the TUR Maori Advisory Group for the New Zealand Sign Language Board.

So we've helped strengthen, um, the group with work they wanted to do around leadership. For example, one of the things in establishing a new agency that I was very mindful of is making sure that the structure had the right roles. And so I was very keen to ensure that Katu chief Advisor Maori, is on the executive team, um, that we have a, who can guide us around the, the tega, um, and approaches for the ministry.

We also, this year, invested in a kaiga, um, Maori researcher and a Maori engagement lead for the New Zealand disability strategy to really help us get into some of the harder to reach areas of New Zealand. I think in terms of your, um, well actually just before I come to your specific question about indicators, um, we are also doing a, a lot of work, um, through Elle's great leadership around, uh, working to increase the ministry's capability around, um, Ang and taking a Maori, um, approach to our work.

So our policy group, for example, did some training on, um, or making sure that we are bringing that perspective into our, um, policy work and around the indicators of success for tga, kaha, Maori, um, where there is data available. We want to be able to, um, break that down in terms of how we measure against those five areas in the strategy.

So that's going to be a, a focus of the work, um, in the new year. Just a Quick one, if I may. Thank you. Uh, I'm, I'm encouraged. Uh, I, I like, and I get, uh, um, connecting to what you're saying, it's encouraging. I, um, Paula, can you, so on the ground, if I was to kind of like drop my eyes and, um, I, people like Patty Poor come up in in mind for me because she's constantly, um, letting me know and that that's great.

And I, I wonder how quickly, or how progressively or how actually these, this will reach them, each wau on the ground, wau and families on the ground. I know it's, I mean, that's like a, well, I don't know mental, but No, but I'm just interested too, and, uh, probably it even connects to the, um, question that our, my colleague asked recently about the, um, the group that, that the advisory group that might be, looks like might not happen or might not exist anymore. So, Yeah. Yeah.

So, so thank you for that. Um, and, and you're right. I mean, these things have to hit the ground for disabled people and for danger Maori and, um, you know, it, it doesn't necessarily provide comfort for people, but the, the reality is we have spent the year, um, really getting these work programs, um, and, and strategies in place.

The critical focus is on implementing those now. And so we are fully in that stage of developing the implementation plan. And I think the thing about the New Zealand disability strategy is there are actions which ministers have already agreed to embedded in it. So once we start delivering on those, then, you know, inevitably we'll take, um, some time for that to, to reach people.

I think, you know, one of the things that, um, we heard a a lot through the development of the strategy was around housing for Taka Maori. And so we, um, again, this year took advantage of a Maori housing conference and started to build some, you know, on the ground connections there around, well, actually how are we going to provide, um, accessible housing for t ha Maori and, and really building awareness and giving support and information to those, um, companies and organizations.

So, um, it's, it's, you know, on all of us across government Yeah. To make sure these things actually make a difference on the ground. Your question about the advisory group, um, like what, um, the member was, um, asking about was the national enabling good lives growth, um, which the minister has determined that, um, the ministry will have that core relationship with NEGL.

Um, but we have other advisory groups, a strategic advisory group and accessibility advisory group, um, and we have t ka Maori equitably represented. Thank you. So Thank you. So just to confirm there, that that Maori advisory board was disestablished, was, is that correct?

Oh, so you're, you're not saying that it was like, um, awesome. That's great to hear because there was a Maori advisory group, sorry, I can't remember the name, that Disestablish at the end of 2023 or the beginning of 2024. I'm not sure what group norm referring to. That's case.

We've really absolutely strengthened the number of, um, groups that we have, the very, very early groups that helped establish the ministry. The composition of those groups no longer exist, but what we have now is a strategic advisory group.

We support the disabled, um, people's, uh, organization, coalition, um, we support the New Zealand sign language board. So there are a range of organizations, um, of Wang Maia represented on. So I'm not aware of a Awesome, I I'll, I, I was like, oh, I'm pretty sure there was one, but I couldn't remember the name.

So I'll go back and, and do written questions later on. Um, just one more supplementary on that. So with the disability strategy that's coming out tomorrow, very exciting, um, and obviously went out to, um, to the public for feedback on that. Is it correct that those focus areas were chosen without consultation with dis disabled people?

And what could, like now in hindsight, what could you have done to improve that consultation period for choosing which, um, which areas would be highlighted in the strategy? So ministers wanted to have a strategy that would focus on a number of areas where demonstrable progress could be made over five years.

So that is really different than, uh, um, previous New Zealand disability strategy, which was over a 10 year period. It was a strategy without actions embedded. Those actions, um, took place. Um, action plans were developed, you know, in subsequent years, but what the minister wanted was areas that we know through multiple sources of feedback, um, and results in terms of outcomes that those five areas are critical to improving for the lives of disabled people.

The strategy is an important piece of our work, but it's just one piece of our work. And so when you then add to that, our accessibility work program, for example, which touches on, um, the built environment, transport, digital accessibility, and emergency management, those are also areas, particularly transport and the built environment that we also heard through the strategy are really important for disabled people.

So when you look in totality across our work program, we believe we are tackling issues of real importance for disabled people. So you are confident some, would You, oh, um, I've had a fair bit of feedback that one of the areas that was raised as an important area through that consultation was living, but that wasn't then chosen as a category.

You've mentioned that ministers wanted actionable change in areas that make a difference to the lives of disabled people. Was that feedback that you received as well on cost of living being an important category? And if so, why was not, what, why did that not make the cut? I think, um, you know, ul ultimately these are decisions for the government in terms of what sits within this strategy.

Uh, I think if you look at some of our underlying things in, in the strategy, there's probably two things. I'd say. One, the strategy is broken into two parts. It has a vision and principles, and those are intended to apply to all things that government does. Not just those five areas, but in terms of the way government delivers and develops its services and policies.

Those vision and principles are intended to apply across the board then with this. Then the second part of the strategy is the specific, um, actions across those five areas. So we know that employment, for example, is a, um, a, a a lever to assist with cost of living, and we've touched on what we are doing in employment and that gap.

So we did receive a wide range of feedback on the strategy. Mm-hmm. And ultimately, what sits in that strategy were those five areas that, um, Did, did you receive feedback On cost of Living being a significant area that people wanted to see change on? That should be a category though, In terms Of not, not a category in its own right.

So, uh, yes, we received feedback around the being an issue that, uh, is important for disabled people at the moment, as it is for, you know, many, many other New Zealanders, um, but not so much that, uh, it should be a, a category. Okay. For action. Yes. Sorry, I'm just gonna some clarity here.

I've heard a few questions where I just haven't heard the answer. One Is, did you vote, because that was a question, um, that came up, and I think the answer was that the priorities were set by the government and you didn't consult because that was their job. Is am I right about So that, so the minister determined, um, with advice from us around the critical areas affecting the lives of disabled people.

Um, and that advice comes from, you know, well understood outcome, poor outcome areas and areas where we need to improve. So the initial, um, consultation was advice based on what we understood and knew then the server, the, um, disability strategy itself was the subject of wide consultation throughout New Zealand. That's your, is that your 10 year plan versus this five year low hanging fruit thing?

No, the 10 year strategy is the previous strategy. Okay. This strategy launched tomorrow is the new refreshed New Zealand disability strategy, which takes the next five years. That Is the five, Yeah. Okay. I've got a, um, uh, there's a couple of questions arising out of your report, um, for the, you know, annual report, 20 24, 25.

Um, so about one in six New Zealand's are to say called the Status New Zealand, uh, 2023 household survey. Uh, I'm curious, how does that compare to sort of similarish countries in terms of that one, one in one in six? Is that, is that, um, relatively standard across countries, or are we, are we sort of the high end, the low end median? You know, It's quite hard to make those sorts of comparisons.

Um, what we do know is that from the 2023 survey, we used measures and a definition that are very well aligned with international best practice in terms of how you measure disability. So we have a, a greater confidence in that one in six. And could could you provide the committees maybe afterwards if it just, just some, you know, comparative information be, be useful?

Yes, by all Means. Thank you. And just, just outta that report, I, I note that, um, uh, some outcomes have improved that disabled people says, uh, comparing census results between 2018 and 2023 shows a reduction in percentage of disabled people with no qualification. Uh, there are increases in percent of disabled children participating in education and percent disabled people employed, including increasing Maori and Pacific disabled people and small improvements also made housing quality for disabled people.

Do, do you have any data on what helped shift that in a positive direction? There will be a range of things. Happy to follow up with some specifics, but I think, um, you know, you are right, there have been, you know, some shifts overall. We know that there is much more work to be done to improve, improve outcomes overall.

Yeah, it'd be, it'd be great if you're able to circle back with any information that can sort of help inform our committee and what things are helping move things in the right direction. Thank you. Thanks. Um, This is, um, oh, sorry, I, I, I had a, a new question from. Great. Thank you. Um, thank you. Uh, your annual report, um, states quite clearly that disabled people are more likely to experience severe housing deprivation compared to those who are non-disabled.

Um, some of the top issues that are raised with me are related to housing. Um, one of that of course is parents of often adult intellectually disabled children, um, who are in residential care facilities. Um, also those young people who are forced into such facilities because there are no alternatives.

Disabled people who would rather flat but have no other choice. Now, F Kaha had a program, my Home, my Choice that was working on security, um, in those residential facilities and outside of it, but also alternatives to residential facilities is that it was funded for four years.

Is that program still ongoing or not? Um, so the responsibility for disability support services, which includes the provision of residential care, moved to MSD, um, as did any work on that program. So FA Kaha is not doing specific work on that program.

Um, it may be a question that the committee would have for MSD. What we are doing is, as a touched on briefly before, just circling back to housing being one of the key outcome areas in the New Zealand disability strategy. So that program has moved to MSD from Faha?
